peer_exemplars.reasoning: High agreeableness and trust in institutions, combined with very high authoritarian submission and conventionalism, support moderate backing for transparency as a way to maintain order and trust, but low openness and libertarian anti-censorship orientation temper enthusiasm for heavy-handed mandates.

While my strong care/harm foundation and moderate anti-censorship lean support transparency to prevent harm, my deeply traditional-authoritarian worldview and very low openness make me skeptical of forcing private platforms to reveal proprietary systems, resulting in mild support.

Despite high trust in institutions and a strong GAL-TAN Green/participatory streak, the person's very low openness, high authoritarianism, dual-process dangerous-worldview, and low libertarian anti-censorship orientation override any transparency impulse, making them skeptical of forcing private platforms to reveal proprietary algorithms.

High libertarian anti-censorship orientation (68.92) and skepticism of state control (74.04) create moderate support for transparency as a check on platform power, but very low agreeableness (0.61) and high authoritarian law-and-order preference (81.36) temper enthusiasm for mandated disclosure.

High extraversion and low agreeableness (HEXACO) combined with strong anti-elitism and distrust of institutions (trust in science at 2.57) produce moderate support for transparency as a check on opaque power, but low trust in government (7.77) tempers enthusiasm for mandated disclosure.
High openness and GAL-TAN values drive strong support for transparency, tempered by low conscientiousness and moderate trust in institutions.
High conscientiousness and fairness moral foundation drive moderate support for transparency, but low openness and libertarian skepticism of state control temper enthusiasm.

Very high openness, high agreeableness, and very strong GAL-TAN green/libertarian values produce firm support for transparency to protect against manipulation, though high authoritarianism slightly tempers the confidence in bottom-up enforcement.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Strong moral foundations for fairness/reciprocity and authority/subversion, plus high trust in police and government, drive clear support for disclosure to preserve social order, though low openness and moderate libertarian skepticism of state control prevent maximal endorsement.

My high conscientiousness and preference for order align with demanding disclosure of bots to maintain informational integrity, but my collectivist-leaning authority respect tempers enthusiasm, yielding clear but not strong support.

Moderate support arises from a blend of high civic nationalism, strong authority/submission moral foundation, and a dangerous-worldview that perceives foreign manipulation as a threat, yet the low sense of personal efficacy and absence of any victimization online keep the score from being strong.

Strong moral foundation of care/harm (76.34) and high harm avoidance (95.76) drive support for protecting audiences from deception, while high authoritarian acceptance of coercive social control (85.23) reinforces endorsement of disclosure mandates.

Very high extraversion and high conscientiousness (Big Five) alongside a dangerous worldview (71.59) and strong nationalism (ethnic nationalism 92.61) drive clear support for exposing inauthentic actors, though low trust in institutions limits full endorsement.
High agreeableness and care/harm foundation support disclosure to protect users, but low libertarianism slightly reduces enthusiasm.
Strong fairness and reciprocity values combined with high dutifulness produce clear support for disclosure, though low trust in institutions prevents maximal endorsement.
High trust in institutions, very high care/harm moral foundation, and strong fact-checking behavior align to robustly support mandatory disclosure as a harm-reduction measure.

Clear opposition emerges because, despite high trust in government, the person's extreme authoritarian submission and powerful collective civic nationalism reject any agency that could be perceived as undermining national unity or usurping local authority, while high political trust ironically makes them see such agencies as unnecessary.

Moderate support is driven by high trust in the judiciary and police, strong civic nationalism, and a belief in accountability (values domain), but is tempered by the person's very low openness and a general suspicion of advertising as a foreign or manipulative influence, making them only moderately enthusiastic.

High libertarian anti-censorship (68.92) and anti-domination (90.15) favor transparency as a check on manipulation, but low trust in government (34.96) and high authoritarianism (76.62) create ambivalence about enforcement.

High conscientiousness (70.56) and strong nationalism (civic nationalism 80.36) combined with a dangerous worldview (71.59) drive clear support for transparency to protect electoral integrity, though low institutional trust tempers enthusiasm for enforcement.
High agreeableness and fairness foundation support transparency, though low authoritarianism slightly reduces punitive aspects.
Fairness and reciprocity drive support, while low openness and moderate authoritarian submission create some resistance, resulting in moderate endorsement.
Strong anti-bullying/tyranny liberty foundation, very high political efficacy, and support for campaign finance transparency combine to solidly back advertising disclosure mandates.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: High authoritarian submission and conventionalism, combined with high trust in institutions and strong authority/subversion moral foundation, drive moderate-to-strong support for regulating platforms to enforce accountability, though low openness and moderate libertarian skepticism prevent maximal support.
My moderate libertarian skepticism of state control and market-oriented economic views lead to mild opposition despite my support for order and fairness.

Clear opposition flows from very high Eysenckian psychoticism (coldness, egocentricity), a Disruptor profile that resists external regulation, and the conviction that platforms are extensions of national sovereignty best governed by informal trust rather than regulatory mandates.

High libertarian skepticism of state control (74.04) and very low agreeableness (0.61) drive opposition to regulation, though high authoritarian law-and-order (81.36) and punitive attitudes (72.85) create some internal tension.

Low trust in government (7.77) and high anti-elitism (52.77) combined with a strong free spirit (67.34) and disruptor (68.94) user type produce mild opposition to regulation, seen as potentially stifling innovation and freedom.
Moderate support due to high trust in institutions but balanced by libertarian skepticism of regulation.
High authoritarian submission and conventionalism are balanced by low trust in institutions and libertarian skepticism, yielding near-neutral ambivalence.
Care/harm and fairness motives strongly favor regulation, but libertarian skepticism of state control and some economic policy conservatism pull the score down from a higher level.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Very high traditional authoritarian nationalism (98.8%), civic nationalism (98.8%), and trust in government (69.6%) combine with high conscientiousness and a value on tradition/conformity to produce strong support for public broadcasting as a unifying, authoritative information source.
Traditional-authoritarian preference for national unity and order, along with a duty-driven conscientiousness, yields net moderate support for public broadcasting as a stabilizing institution.

Strong support is driven by very high trust in government (81.31) and parliament (87.67), high civic nationalism, and a traditional-authoritarian respect for authoritative institutions that should produce unifying content, despite the person's low openness and personal disengagement from news-seeking.

Low trust in government (34.96) and high libertarian anti-censorship (68.92) oppose state-funded media, reinforced by very low agreeableness (0.61) and high ethnic nationalism (91.44) that may view public broadcasting as elite-driven.

Extremely low trust in government (7.77) and science (2.57) combined with high anti-elitism (52.77) and a strong competitive worldview (87.64) generate clear opposition to funding a state-backed media entity perceived as an elite institution.
High trust in institutions and GAL-TAN values strongly support public broadcasting, despite low authoritarianism.
Moderate support from high dutifulness and civic engagement is weakened by low trust in media and economic conservatism, resulting in mild endorsement.
Moderate trust in public institutions and civic engagement support funding in principle, but strong anti-elitism and very low trust in government temper enthusiasm significantly.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Low openness to experience and high authoritarian submission create mild opposition to giving users choice over algorithms, seen as potentially undermining social order and exposing people to harmful content, despite moderate digital literacy.
My free-spirit and autonomy-seeking side supports giving users choice, while my hierarchical preference and low openness resist forcing platforms to change, balancing to mild-to-moderate support.

Mild opposition emerges from a combination of very low openness (9.22), high conscientiousness (69.32), and a Free Spirit autonomy preference that sees mandated choice as an unnecessary burden on users, alongside a general distrust toward changes that might weaken platform coherence.

High libertarian anti-censorship (68.92) and free spirit exploration (76.39) support user choice, but low openness to experience (22.69) and high authoritarian conformity (67.35) reduce enthusiasm for system-level changes.

High extraversion (98.32) and free spirit autonomy (67.25) combined with moderate openness (57.59) produce mild support for giving users control over algorithms, though low trust in regulators to implement this effectively limits stronger endorsement.
Moderate support from high openness and autonomy values, but low conscientiousness may limit perceived need for mandates.
Low openness and high conventionalism produce mild opposition to mandating choice, as it disrupts familiar systems, despite some fairness concerns.
Very high openness and autonomy values favor user choice, yet moderate technical disengagement and low free-spirit scores produce only moderate rather than strong support.

Strong sanctity/purity concerns and a desire to protect informational order drive clear support for labelling synthetic media, moderated slightly by my anti-censorship bent.

Clear support comes from a strong dangerous-worldview (93.47) and high group-based dominance (45.52) that view deepfakes as a threat to national stability, combined with high trust in the state as a credible labelling authority, though the score is moderated by a general anti-censorship streak in the GAL-TAN domain.

High conscientiousness (70.56) and a dangerous worldview (71.59) combined with strong care/harm foundation (93.03) drive moderate support for labelling synthetic media as a protective measure, though libertarian anti-censorship leanings (57.37) prevent stronger support.
High openness and care/harm foundation strongly support labeling to prevent harm, tempered by low authoritarianism.
Strong fairness and reciprocity values, plus high conscientiousness, drive clear support for labelling, though low openness tempers enthusiasm for new mandates.
Strong honesty-humility and integrity values combine with high care/harm to support labeling to prevent deception, although low trust in enforcement institutions prevents a higher score.

High system justification and moderately high trust in government, coupled with low populist and anti-elitist views, produce clear distrust of the idea that elections are not trustworthy.

Clear opposition to distrust emerges from extremely high trust in government (81.31), judiciary (99.46), and police (97.37), paired with high external political efficacy (82.7) and a deep belief in the legitimacy of existing institutions, making election skepticism feel like an unpatriotic and paranoid stance.

Despite low trust in government (34.96) and low external efficacy (37.35), very high system justification (72.26) and high authoritarian law-and-order (81.36) produce clear opposition to election distrust, as it threatens institutional stability.

Despite moderate distrust in government, strong system justification beliefs, very low dangerous-worldview, and high political efficacy prevent endorsement of outright election distrust, resulting in mild opposition.
